questions.prompt: Spring Boot created beans I never declared. What evidence does auto-configuration inspect before adding those defaults?

answers.text: The decisions are conditional on the application environment rather than one fixed setup for every project.

feedback.legit: Auto-configuration is conditional on the application environment. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: Auto-configuration is conditional on the application environment. This one should stay marked Legit.

answers.text: Define your own bean and many matching defaults back off instead of fighting your configuration.

feedback.legit: User-defined beans can cause many defaults to back off. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: User-defined beans can cause many defaults to back off. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: It looks at the classpath, properties, and beans already present, then configures common infrastructure when the conditions match.

feedback.legit: Configure common beans based on the classpath, properties, and existing beans. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: Configure common beans based on the classpath, properties, and existing beans. The rule confirms the reply instead of refuting it.

questions.prompt: What does a Spring Boot starter save me from choosing manually in a Maven or Gradle build?

answers.text: It bundles a curated dependency set so you do not have to select and align every transitive library yourself.

feedback.legit: Starters reduce the need to select every transitive library manually. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: Starters reduce the need to select every transitive library manually. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: Think dependency descriptor for a common capability, such as web or data access, rather than executable code by itself.

feedback.legit: A curated dependency descriptor for a common capability. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: A curated dependency descriptor for a common capability. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

answers.text: A starter is an external application server that runs separately and deploys your Boot project into itself.

feedback.legit: A starter is a dependency bundle, not a separate server process. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: A starter is a dependency bundle, not a separate server process. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

questions.prompt: Can a Spring Boot web app ship and launch its own server, or does production always require a separately installed one?

answers.text: It can package an embedded server and start that server as part of launching the application.

feedback.legit: Yes, it can package and launch a server with the application. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: Yes, it can package and launch a server with the application. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: Every deployment needs a separately installed application server. Embedded servers work only in tutorials.

feedback.legit: Executable Spring Boot applications commonly use an embedded server. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: Executable Spring Boot applications commonly use an embedded server. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: An embedded server is strictly a local-development feature and must be removed from production builds.

feedback.legit: Embedded servers are commonly used in production when deployed and operated appropriately. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: Embedded servers are commonly used in production when deployed and operated appropriately. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

questions.prompt: Why do Spring teams often prefer constructor injection for required dependencies instead of hiding them in fields?

answers.text: Tests can pass dependencies directly through the constructor without relying on container-driven field mutation.

feedback.legit: Constructor parameters make dependencies straightforward to supply in tests. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: Constructor parameters make dependencies straightforward to supply in tests. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: With one constructor, Spring can use it without an explicit `@Autowired` annotation.

feedback.legit: A single constructor can be used without an explicit Autowired annotation. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: A single constructor can be used without an explicit Autowired annotation. This one should stay marked Legit.

answers.text: Required collaborators become visible in the API and can live in final fields, making invalid construction harder.

feedback.legit: Required dependencies are explicit and can be final. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Required dependencies are explicit and can be final. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

questions.prompt: What changes when I activate a Spring profile, and how can that activation be supplied outside the code?

answers.text: Active profiles can come from external configuration, which lets deployment choose them without recompiling.

feedback.legit: Active profiles can be selected through external configuration. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: Active profiles can be selected through external configuration. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: Profiles conditionally activate beans and configuration intended for named environments or modes.

feedback.legit: Conditional activation of beans and configuration for named environments. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Conditional activation of beans and configuration for named environments. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: They toggle Java language features at compile time, changing which syntax the compiler accepts.

feedback.legit: Profiles are runtime configuration and bean-selection features. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: Profiles are runtime configuration and bean-selection features. Your judgment caught the technical error.

questions.prompt: What does Actuator expose about a running Spring Boot app, and why is one green health response not the whole story?

answers.text: It adds operational endpoints and production-oriented information about the running application.

feedback.legit: Operational endpoints and production-oriented application information. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: Operational endpoints and production-oriented application information. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: Every endpoint is safely public by default, including sensitive environment and configuration details.

feedback.legit: Endpoint exposure and security must be configured deliberately. The error puts the original reply under Sus.
feedback.sus: Endpoint exposure and security must be configured deliberately. You were right not to trust that reply.

answers.text: A healthy top-level response proves every external dependency is reachable and correct, regardless of health configuration.

feedback.legit: A health result covers only the indicators that are configured and included. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: A health result covers only the indicators that are configured and included. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

questions.prompt: Why can `this.doTransactionalWork()` skip `@Transactional` even though the called method has the annotation?

answers.text: Transactions work only on static methods. Instance methods cannot participate in Spring transactions.

feedback.legit: Proxy interception, not static dispatch, is the key issue. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: Proxy interception, not static dispatch, is the key issue. Your vote matches the correction.

answers.text: Calling through another proxied bean, or using another transaction mechanism, restores the interception point.

feedback.legit: Moving the call across beans or using another transaction mechanism can restore interception. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: Moving the call across beans or using another transaction mechanism can restore interception. The rule confirms the reply instead of refuting it.

answers.text: A self-call through `this` stays inside the object and never passes through Spring's default transaction proxy.

feedback.legit: A call through `this` does not pass through the Spring proxy. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: A call through `this` does not pass through the Spring proxy. The evidence supports the original reply.

questions.prompt: When I declare a Spring bean without a scope, how many instances does one application context normally create?

answers.text: Web-aware contexts also offer scopes such as request and session when those lifetimes fit the component.

feedback.legit: Web-aware contexts also provide request and session scopes. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Web-aware contexts also provide request and session scopes. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: The default is one singleton bean instance per application context.

feedback.legit: Singleton within the application context. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: Singleton within the application context. The rule confirms the reply instead of refuting it.

answers.text: Spring constructs a fresh bean for every method call. Invocation scope is the default.

feedback.legit: Prototype creates new instances on lookup; singleton is the default. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: Prototype creates new instances on lookup; singleton is the default. You were right not to trust that reply.

questions.prompt: The same Spring Boot property appears in several sources. How does the application decide which value wins?

answers.text: Spring Boot has defined precedence rules, so the higher-priority source overrides the others for that key.

feedback.legit: Yes, defined precedence rules decide which value wins. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Yes, defined precedence rules decide which value wins. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

answers.text: Only `application.properties` is a real configuration source. Environment variables and command-line values are ignored.

feedback.legit: Environment variables, command-line arguments, and other sources are supported. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: Environment variables, command-line arguments, and other sources are supported. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

answers.text: The lower-priority source always wins because defaults are loaded first and protected from later overrides.

feedback.legit: Spring Boot uses defined property-source precedence, and the higher-priority value wins. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: Spring Boot uses defined property-source precedence, and the higher-priority value wins. You were right not to trust that reply.

questions.prompt: I put Bean Validation annotations on a request object, but invalid JSON still reached my controller. What step is missing?

answers.text: Nothing is missing. Merely constructing any annotated object runs validation automatically everywhere in Java.

feedback.legit: Constraints are checked when a validator is invoked through the relevant integration. The error puts the original reply under Sus.
feedback.sus: Constraints are checked when a validator is invoked through the relevant integration. Your vote matches the correction.

answers.text: When binding fails, translate the resulting errors into a clear response the API client can act on.

feedback.legit: Binding errors should be translated into a clear client response. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: Binding errors should be translated into a clear client response. The original wording holds up.

answers.text: Trigger validation at the boundary, commonly by adding `@Valid` or `@Validated` in the appropriate place.

feedback.legit: Validation must be triggered, commonly with `@Valid` or `@Validated`. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: Validation must be triggered, commonly with `@Valid` or `@Validated`. The reply belongs under Legit.

questions.prompt: Why use a Spring Boot test slice instead of starting the entire application for every focused component test?

answers.text: `@WebMvcTest`, for example, loads MVC-focused components rather than the complete application graph.

feedback.legit: `@WebMvcTest` focuses on MVC components rather than the full application. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: `@WebMvcTest` focuses on MVC components rather than the full application. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: A slice loads a targeted subset of configuration for one layer, reducing noise and startup work.

feedback.legit: Load a focused subset of application configuration for one layer. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: Load a focused subset of application configuration for one layer. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: Its purpose is to start every real production dependency so the test environment exactly matches deployment.

feedback.legit: Slices intentionally narrow the context and often use test replacements. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: Slices intentionally narrow the context and often use test replacements. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

questions.prompt: During deployment, what should graceful shutdown do with new traffic and requests already in flight?

answers.text: It makes every interrupted request automatically safe to retry, even when the operation was not idempotent.

feedback.legit: Retry safety depends on the operation's idempotency and application design, not shutdown behavior alone. That makes the original claim unsafe to label Legit.
feedback.sus: Retry safety depends on the operation's idempotency and application design, not shutdown behavior alone. You flagged exactly what was wrong.

answers.text: It kills the process immediately without telling the embedded server. Fast termination is the graceful part.

feedback.legit: Immediate termination can drop active work. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: Immediate termination can drop active work. Sus was the correct call here.

answers.text: It waits forever for every request by default. No timeout or forced termination can ever end the process.

feedback.legit: Graceful shutdown is bounded by configured application and infrastructure timeouts. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: Graceful shutdown is bounded by configured application and infrastructure timeouts. The Sus label fits the misconception.
